sources.notes: Read the rooted-tree good-pair definition, triangle inequalities, and constraints: n <= 300000 and sum n <= 300000.
Official hint derives f(u,v)=2*min(dist(u,lca),dist(v,lca))-1; the embedded accepted C++ confirms the linear depth-count, subtree-size, and child-pair formula.
Consulted an independent O(n) edge/threshold contribution derivation using DFS order, depth-layer counts, and subtree sizes.

Consulted the full DSU-on-tree plus augmented FHQ-treap implementation for the materially different depth-multiset solution; its displayed min/max variable typo was corrected against the official formula and code.
Independent write-up cross-checks the O(n) decomposition into minimum-depth, LCA-depth, and good-pair-count contributions.

annotation.summary: For every unordered pair of vertices that are incomparable in the rooted tree, count integer third side lengths forming a non-degenerate triangle with their two branch distances to the LCA, and sum those counts.
annotation.constraints: Up to 10000 tests, 1 <= n <= 300000, and the sum of n is at most 300000, requiring linear or near-linear processing with 64-bit totals.
annotation.core_idea: For a good pair with branch lengths a,b >= 1, triangle inequalities allow exactly 2*min(a,b)-1 integer values. With depths dep and l=lca(u,v), this is 2*min(dep[u],dep[v])-2*dep[l]-1. Let sz[u] be subtree size, cnt[d] the number of vertices at depth d, and ge[d] the suffix count at depths at least d. Summing 2*dep[u]*(ge[dep[u]]-sz[u]) counts every unequal-depth good pair once from its shallower endpoint and every equal-depth pair twice; subtract sum_d d*cnt[d]*(cnt[d]-1) to remove the duplicate equal-depth orientation. For each l, sum_{i<j} sz[child_i]*sz[child_j] pairs have LCA l, so subtract (2*dep[l]+1) times that count. DFS plus depth suffix sums evaluates every term in linear time.
annotation.complexity: O(n) time and O(n) memory per test case.
tricks: Count integer x directly from |a-b| < x < a+b to obtain 2*min(a,b)-1.
Orient the minimum-depth term by its shallower endpoint and correct only equal-depth double counting.
Vertices outside u's subtree with depth at least dep[u] are exactly the possible opposite endpoints counted from u.
Count pairs with a fixed LCA by multiplying sizes of distinct child subtrees.

solution_variants.name: linear-depth-subtree-decomposition
solution_variants.summary: Compute depths, subtree sizes, depth frequencies/suffix counts, and cross-child pair counts; combine the global minimum-depth term with per-LCA corrections using closed formulas.

solution_variants.name: dsu-on-tree-depth-multiset
solution_variants.summary: At each node, count pairs from different child subtrees by querying a new child's depths against the kept depths of earlier children. An augmented ordered treap returns sum min(d,x) from prefix counts/sums, while DSU-on-tree keeps the heavy child's structure, adds light subtrees, and lazily shifts distances when moving to the parent.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n log^2 n) expected time and O(n) memory with a standard augmented treap

tags.evidence: The solution classifies incomparable pairs by rooted subtrees and their LCA, and all aggregate counts come from depths and child-subtree sizes.
The pair value is decomposed into a minimum-depth contribution and an LCA/constant correction, each accumulated globally without enumerating pairs.
A suffix sum of depth frequencies supplies ge[d], the number of vertices at depth at least d, in O(1) per vertex.
Postorder DFS computes depths, subtree sizes, and cross-child pair totals used by every closed-form contribution.
Strict triangle inequalities reduce the allowed integer interval for x to |a-b| < x < a+b and hence to 2*min(a,b)-1 values.
The alternative keeps the heavy child's depth set and reprocesses only light subtrees to amortize cross-subtree pair queries.
The alternative uses an augmented ordered treap with split, count, and sum aggregates to evaluate sums of min(current_depth,stored_depth).
LCA is central to the proof and pair classification, but the primary implementation never preprocesses or answers LCA queries.
The primary linear solution is a fixed contribution formula over DFS statistics, not a dynamic program with competing states or transitions.
